expanded_description: Arc length and sector area are fundamental applications of radian measure that connect angle measurement to linear and area measurements in circles. Arc length is the distance along a circle's circumference subtended by a central angle. Sector area is the area of the 'pie slice' bounded by two radii and an arc. Both formulas require radian measure—this is why radians are essential in mathematics and why they simplify formulas. In Pre-calculus, mastering arc length and sector area develops understanding of the relationship between angles and lengths, prepares students for calculus (where these formulas generalize), and enables solving real-world problems in engineering, physics, and navigation. Linear and angular velocity extend these concepts to motion—how fast a point moves along a circular path (linear velocity) versus how fast the angle changes (angular velocity). These concepts are essential for understanding rotational motion, gear systems, wheels, and any circular motion application. Understanding that s = rθ and A = ½r²θ only work with radians explains why radians are the 'natural' angle measure—they directly relate angles to lengths without conversion factors. This section teaches both the formulas and their applications—how to calculate arc length and sector area, how to find linear and angular velocity, and how to apply these concepts to real-world problems. Mastery enables solving problems involving circular motion, gear design, navigation, and any application where circular measurements matter.

core_concept.principle:
Arc Length:
s = rθ
Where:
- s = arc length (distance along circumference)
- r = radius of circle
- θ = central angle in RADIANS

Sector Area:
A = ½r²θ
Where:
- A = area of sector (pie slice)
- r = radius of circle
- θ = central angle in RADIANS

Linear Velocity:
v = rω
Where:
- v = linear velocity (distance/time)
- r = radius
- ω = angular velocity (radians/time)

Angular Velocity:
ω = θ/t or ω = Δθ/Δt
Where:
- ω = angular velocity (radians per unit time)
- θ = angle rotated
- t = time

Key Requirements:
- θ MUST be in radians for all formulas
- If given degrees, convert to radians first
- Units must be consistent (r and s in same units)

Relationships:
- Arc length is fraction of circumference: s/C = θ/2π
- Sector area is fraction of circle area: A/(πr²) = θ/2π
core_concept.explanation: Think of arc length and sector area as 'portions' of a circle—arc length is a portion of the circumference, sector area is a portion of the total area. The fraction is θ/2π (angle divided by full circle). This is why the formulas work: s = (θ/2π) × 2πr = rθ, and A = (θ/2π) × πr² = ½r²θ. The beauty of radians is that they make these formulas simple—no extra conversion factors needed. If you used degrees, the formulas would be messy: s = (θ°/360°) × 2πr. Radians are 'natural' because they're defined by the circle itself (arc length/radius). Linear velocity (how fast a point moves along the circle) relates to angular velocity (how fast the angle changes) by v = rω—bigger radius means faster linear speed for same angular speed. This explains why outer edge of a wheel moves faster than points near the center. In applications, these formulas solve real problems—how far does a wheel roll, how much area does a sprinkler cover, how fast does a point on a gear move. Understanding these concepts builds foundation for calculus (arc length of curves) and physics (rotational motion). This section teaches the formulas, why radians matter, and how to apply them.

image.alt: Overview of arc length and sector area showing formulas and relationships
image.caption: Figure 7.2.1: Visual summary of arc length (s = rθ), sector area (A = ½r²θ), and linear/angular velocity (v = rω) with diagrams and formulas.

sections.introduction: Arc length is the distance along a circle's circumference between two points. The formula s = rθ relates arc length to radius and central angle—but θ must be in radians. This formula is fundamental for calculating distances along circular paths, from wheels rolling to satellites orbiting. Understanding arc length develops understanding of the relationship between angles and lengths.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.2.2: Introducing arc length formula s = rθ with diagram.

key_concept.definition:
Arc Length Formula:
s = rθ

Where:
- s = arc length (same units as r)
- r = radius of circle
- θ = central angle in RADIANS

Derivation:
Full circumference: C = 2πr
Full angle: 2π radians
Fraction of circle: θ/2π
Arc length: s = (θ/2π) × 2πr = rθ

Important:
- θ MUST be in radians
- If given degrees: convert first (× π/180)
- s and r must have same units
- Result s has same units as r

Applications:
- Distance wheel rolls (s = rθ)
- Satellite orbit arc
- Pendulum swing distance
- Gear tooth travel
- Navigation (great circle distance)

Special Cases:
- θ = 2π: s = 2πr (full circumference)
- θ = π: s = πr (semicircle)
- θ = π/2: s = πr/2 (quarter circle)
key_concept.context: Arc length is practical—how far does a wheel roll in one rotation? How far does a satellite travel along its orbit? How much cable is needed for a curved bridge? The formula s = rθ is elegant and simple—but only with radians. This is why radians matter—they make formulas work cleanly. In engineering, arc length calculates material needed for curved parts. In physics, it calculates distance traveled in circular motion. In navigation, it calculates distance along Earth's surface (great circle arcs). Understanding arc length builds understanding of circular geometry and prepares for calculus (arc length of curves). The requirement for radians is crucial—using degrees gives wrong answers. This section teaches arc length—formula, derivation, applications, and why radians matter.

image.caption: Figure 7.2.3: Diagram showing arc length s = rθ, with radius r, angle θ, and arc s highlighted.

worked_examples.title: Basic Arc Length Calculation

image.alt: Example of basic arc length calculation
image.caption: Figure 7.2.4: Calculating arc length with given radius and angle.

worked_examples.problem:
A circle has radius 8 cm. Find the arc length subtended by a central angle of:
a) π/4 radians
b) 60°

step_by_step.explanation: Use s = rθ with θ in radians.
Use s = rθ with θ in radians.

step_by_step.calculation: Radius in cm, arc length in cm ✓

Arc length is distance along circle's edge
π/4 rad (45°): 6.28 cm
π/3 rad (60°): 8.38 cm

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 2π cm ≈ 6.28 cm; b) 8π/3 cm ≈ 8.38 cm
worked_examples.concept_applied: Arc length: s = rθ (θ must be in radians); convert degrees to radians first.

worked_examples.title: Arc Length with Large Angle

image.alt: Example of arc length with large angle
image.caption: Figure 7.2.5: Calculating arc length for angle greater than 2π.

worked_examples.problem: A wheel with radius 0.3 meters rotates through 5π radians. How far does a point on the edge travel?

step_by_step.explanation: 5π radians is more than one full rotation.

step_by_step.calculation: Point on edge travels 4.71 meters along circular path

worked_examples.concept_applied: Arc length works for any angle, including angles > 2π; s = rθ gives total distance traveled.

worked_examples.title: Real-World Application: Wheel Distance

image.caption: Figure 7.2.6: Calculating distance a wheel rolls using arc length.

worked_examples.problem: A bicycle wheel has radius 35 cm. How far does the bicycle travel when the wheel rotates through 10 complete revolutions?

step_by_step.explanation: Use s = rθ with r = 35 cm.
Convert cm to meters for practical answer.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Wheel distance: arc length = rθ; one revolution = 2π radians; distance = revolutions × circumference.

image.caption: Figure 7.2.7: Summary of arc length formula, applications, and key points.

sections.introduction: Sector area is the area of the 'pie slice' of a circle bounded by two radii and an arc. The formula A = ½r²θ relates sector area to radius and central angle—but θ must be in radians. This formula is fundamental for calculating areas of circular regions, from sprinkler coverage to pizza slices. Understanding sector area develops understanding of the relationship between angles and areas.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.2.8: Introducing sector area formula A = ½r²θ with diagram.

key_concept.definition:
Sector Area Formula:
A = ½r²θ

Where:
- A = sector area (square units)
- r = radius of circle
- θ = central angle in RADIANS

Derivation:
Full circle area: πr²
Full angle: 2π radians
Fraction of circle: θ/2π
Sector area: A = (θ/2π) × πr² = ½r²θ

Important:
- θ MUST be in radians
- If given degrees: convert first (× π/180)
- r must be in consistent units
- Result A is in square units

Applications:
- Sprinkler coverage area
- Pizza slice area
- Pie chart sectors
- Circular garden sections
- Radar sweep area

Special Cases:
- θ = 2π: A = πr² (full circle)
- θ = π: A = ½πr² (semicircle)
- θ = π/2: A = ¼πr² (quarter circle)
key_concept.context: Sector area is practical—how much area does a sprinkler cover? How big is a pizza slice? How much of a pie chart represents a category? The formula A = ½r²θ is elegant and simple—but only with radians. This is why radians matter—they make formulas work cleanly. In agriculture, sector area calculates irrigation coverage. In food service, it calculates portion sizes. In data visualization, it calculates pie chart sectors. Understanding sector area builds understanding of circular geometry and prepares for calculus (area under curves). The requirement for radians is crucial—using degrees gives wrong answers. This section teaches sector area—formula, derivation, applications, and why radians matter.

image.caption: Figure 7.2.9: Diagram showing sector area A = ½r²θ, with radius r, angle θ, and sector area highlighted.

worked_examples.title: Basic Sector Area Calculation

image.alt: Example of basic sector area calculation
image.caption: Figure 7.2.10: Calculating sector area with given radius and angle.

worked_examples.problem:
A circle has radius 10 inches. Find the area of a sector with central angle of:
a) π/3 radians
b) 90°

step_by_step.explanation: Use A = ½r²θ with θ in radians.
Use A = ½r²θ with θ in radians.

step_by_step.calculation: Radius in inches, area in square inches ✓

Sector area is area of pie slice
π/3 rad (60°): 52.36 in²
π/2 rad (90°): 78.54 in²

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 50π/3 in² ≈ 52.36 in²; b) 25π in² ≈ 78.54 in²
worked_examples.concept_applied: Sector area: A = ½r²θ (θ must be in radians); convert degrees to radians first.

worked_examples.title: Sprinkler Coverage Application

image.caption: Figure 7.2.11: Calculating sprinkler coverage area using sector area.

worked_examples.problem: A lawn sprinkler sprays water in a circular pattern with radius 15 feet. If the sprinkler rotates through an angle of 120°, what area of lawn does it water?

step_by_step.explanation: Use A = ½r²θ with r = 15 ft.

step_by_step.calculation: Sprinkler waters approximately 236 square feet of lawn

step_by_step.explanation: Real sprinklers may have overlap or gaps.
step_by_step.calculation: This is ideal coverage; actual may vary

worked_examples.concept_applied: Sector area for sprinkler coverage: A = ½r²θ; convert angle to radians; verify with fraction method.

worked_examples.title: Pizza Slice Area Comparison

image.alt: Example of pizza slice area comparison
image.caption: Figure 7.2.12: Comparing pizza slice areas using sector area.

worked_examples.problem: A large pizza has diameter 16 inches and is cut into 8 equal slices. A small pizza has diameter 12 inches and is cut into 6 equal slices. Which pizza has larger slices?

step_by_step.calculation:
Large slice: 8π ≈ 25.13 in²
Small slice: 6π ≈ 18.85 in²
Large pizza has larger slices
Even though small pizza has fewer slices, large pizza slices are bigger due to larger radius

worked_examples.final_answer: Large pizza has larger slices: 8π in² ≈ 25.13 in² vs. 6π in² ≈ 18.85 in²
worked_examples.concept_applied: Sector area for pizza slices: A = ½r²θ; compare areas to determine which is larger.

image.caption: Figure 7.2.13: Summary of sector area formula, applications, and key points.

sections.introduction: Linear and angular velocity describe motion along circular paths. Angular velocity (ω) measures how fast an angle changes (radians per unit time). Linear velocity (v) measures how fast a point moves along the circular path (distance per unit time). The relationship v = rω connects them—bigger radius means faster linear speed for same angular speed. These concepts are essential for understanding rotational motion in physics and engineering.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to linear and angular velocity
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.2.14: Introducing linear velocity (v) and angular velocity (ω) with relationship v = rω.

key_concept.definition:
Angular Velocity:
ω = θ/t or ω = Δθ/Δt
Where:
- ω = angular velocity (radians per unit time)
- θ = angle rotated (radians)
- t = time

Linear Velocity:
v = s/t or v = rω
Where:
- v = linear velocity (distance per unit time)
- s = arc length traveled
- r = radius
- ω = angular velocity (radians per unit time)

Relationship:
v = rω (linear velocity = radius × angular velocity)

Important:
- ω MUST be in radians per unit time
- If given revolutions per minute (RPM), convert: ω = RPM × 2π/60
- Units must be consistent

Applications:
- Wheel speed (cars, bicycles)
- Gear systems
- Rotating machinery
- Satellite orbits
- Centrifuges

Key Insight:
For same ω, larger r means larger v
Outer edge of wheel moves faster than inner points
key_concept.context: Linear and angular velocity are fundamental to rotational motion. In a car, the wheels rotate (angular velocity) and the car moves forward (linear velocity). In a gear system, different sized gears have different linear speeds at their edges but same angular speed where they mesh. In physics, this relationship explains why outer planets move slower than inner planets (same angular speed would mean different linear speeds). Understanding v = rω builds understanding of rotational dynamics and prepares for physics courses. The requirement for radians is crucial—angular velocity in degrees/second won't work in v = rω. This section teaches linear and angular velocity—definitions, relationship, applications, and why radians matter.

image.caption: Figure 7.2.15: Diagram showing relationship v = rω, with radius r, angular velocity ω, and linear velocity v.

worked_examples.title: Basic Velocity Calculation

image.caption: Figure 7.2.16: Calculating linear velocity from angular velocity.

worked_examples.problem: A wheel with radius 0.4 meters rotates at 10 radians per second. Find the linear velocity of a point on the edge.

step_by_step.calculation: Point on edge moves at 4 meters per second along circular path

step_by_step.explanation: Distinguish between angular and linear velocity.
step_by_step.calculation:
Angular: 10 rad/s (how fast angle changes)
Linear: 4 m/s (how fast point moves)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Linear velocity: v = rω; angular velocity must be in radians per unit time.

worked_examples.title: Converting RPM to Angular Velocity

image.alt: Example converting RPM to angular velocity
image.caption: Figure 7.2.17: Converting revolutions per minute to radians per second.

worked_examples.problem: A motor rotates at 1200 RPM (revolutions per minute). Find the angular velocity in radians per second and the linear velocity of a point 0.2 meters from the center.

step_by_step.step: Convert RPM to Radians per Second

step_by_step.calculation:
Angular velocity: 40π rad/s ≈ 125.66 rad/s
Linear velocity at r=0.2m: 8π m/s ≈ 25.13 m/s

worked_examples.final_answer: Angular velocity: 40π rad/s ≈ 125.66 rad/s; Linear velocity: 8π m/s ≈ 25.13 m/s
worked_examples.concept_applied: RPM to rad/s: ω = RPM × 2π/60; then v = rω for linear velocity.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Velocities at Different Radii

image.alt: Example comparing velocities at different radii
image.caption: Figure 7.2.18: Comparing linear velocities at different radii for same angular velocity.

worked_examples.problem:
A record player rotates at 33⅓ RPM. Find the linear velocity of a point at:
a) 5 cm from center
b) 15 cm from center

step_by_step.step: Convert RPM to Radians per Second

step_by_step.explanation: Use v = rω with r = 0.05 m.
Use v = rω with r = 0.15 m.

step_by_step.calculation:
At 5 cm: 17.5 cm/s
At 15 cm: 52.4 cm/s
Outer point moves 3× faster (radius is 3× larger)

step_by_step.explanation: Explain why outer points move faster.
step_by_step.calculation:
Same angular velocity, but larger radius means longer path per rotation
v = rω: linear velocity proportional to radius

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 17.5 cm/s; b) 52.4 cm/s; Outer point moves 3× faster
worked_examples.concept_applied: Linear velocity proportional to radius: v = rω; same ω, larger r means larger v.

image.caption: Figure 7.2.19: Summary of linear and angular velocity, formulas, and applications.

sections.introduction: Arc length, sector area, and velocity concepts have numerous real-world applications in engineering, physics, navigation, and everyday life. Understanding these applications shows why the mathematics matters—these aren't just abstract formulas but tools for solving real problems. From calculating how far a car travels to designing gear systems to planning satellite orbits, these concepts are essential.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.2.20: Introducing real-world applications of arc length, sector area, and velocity.

key_concept.definition:
Engineering Applications:
- Gear design: tooth travel distance (arc length)
- Belt drives: belt length around pulleys
- Cam design: follower motion
- Structural engineering: curved beam lengths

Physics Applications:
- Circular motion: distance traveled
- Rotational dynamics: work and energy
- Orbital mechanics: satellite paths
- Pendulum motion: swing arc

Navigation Applications:
- Great circle navigation: distance along Earth's surface
- Radar sweep: area covered
- Sonar: detection range sectors

Everyday Applications:
- Bicycle/scooter: distance per wheel rotation
- Sprinkler systems: coverage area
- Pizza/food: portion sizes
- Clocks: hand movement distance

Problem-Solving Strategy:
1. Identify what's given (r, θ, t, etc.)
2. Determine what's needed (s, A, v, ω)
3. Choose appropriate formula
4. Ensure θ is in radians
5. Check units are consistent
6. Calculate and interpret result
key_concept.context: Applications show why we learn these formulas—they solve real problems. In engineering, arc length calculates material needed for curved parts. In physics, velocity formulas describe rotational motion. In navigation, great circle arcs calculate shortest paths on Earth. In everyday life, these formulas help understand bicycle travel, sprinkler coverage, food portions. Understanding applications builds appreciation for mathematics as practical tool. The requirement for radians is crucial in all applications—using degrees gives wrong answers. This section teaches applications—how to recognize which formula to use, how to set up problems, and how to interpret results in context.

image.caption: Figure 7.2.21: Diagram showing applications in engineering, physics, navigation, and everyday life.

worked_examples.title: Gear System Application

image.caption: Figure 7.2.22: Calculating gear tooth travel using arc length.

worked_examples.problem: Two gears mesh together. Gear A has radius 4 cm and rotates through π/2 radians. Gear B has radius 6 cm. How far does a tooth on each gear travel, and through what angle does Gear B rotate?

step_by_step.step: Calculate Arc Length for Gear A
step_by_step.explanation: Use s = rθ for Gear A.
Meshing gears have same arc length at contact point.
Use s = rθ solved for θ: θ = s/r.
Explain what this means for gear system.
step_by_step.calculation:
Gear A tooth travels 6.28 cm
Gear B tooth travels same 6.28 cm
Gear B rotates through π/3 rad (60°)
Smaller gear rotates more for same tooth travel

worked_examples.final_answer: Both teeth travel 2π cm ≈ 6.28 cm; Gear B rotates π/3 radians (60°)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Meshing gears: same arc length at contact; θ = s/r to find angle; smaller gear rotates more.

worked_examples.title: Satellite Orbit Application

image.caption: Figure 7.2.23: Calculating satellite travel distance using arc length.

worked_examples.problem: A satellite orbits Earth at altitude 400 km (Earth radius ≈ 6371 km). If it travels through a central angle of 0.1 radians, how far does it travel along its orbit?

step_by_step.explanation: Use s = rθ with θ = 0.1 radians.
Confirm θ is in radians (given as 0.1 rad).

step_by_step.calculation: Satellite travels 677.1 km along its orbital path

step_by_step.explanation: Real orbits are elliptical, not perfectly circular.
step_by_step.calculation: This is approximate; actual orbit may vary

worked_examples.concept_applied: Satellite arc length: s = rθ with orbit radius; θ must be in radians.

worked_examples.title: Bicycle Speed Application

image.caption: Figure 7.2.24: Calculating bicycle speed using wheel rotation and linear velocity.

worked_examples.problem: A bicycle wheel has radius 35 cm. If the wheel rotates at 2 revolutions per second, how fast is the bicycle moving (in km/h)?

step_by_step.step: Convert RPM to Radians per Second

step_by_step.explanation: Distance per revolution × revolutions per second.

step_by_step.calculation: Bicycle is moving at approximately 15.8 km/h

worked_examples.concept_applied: Bicycle speed: v = rω or v = circumference × rev/s; convert units appropriately.

image.caption: Figure 7.2.25: Summary of real-world applications of arc length, sector area, and velocity.

sections.section_title: 7.2.5 Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Arc length, sector area, and velocity calculations introduce specific pitfalls related to radian/degree confusion, unit inconsistencies, formula misuse, and interpretation errors. Recognizing common mistakes helps students develop careful habits and produce accurate results. Understanding where errors typically occur enables self-correction and better problem-solving.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.2.26: Overview of common errors in arc length, sector area, and velocity calculations.

key_concept.definition:
Common Errors:

1. Radian/Degree Confusion:
- Using degrees in s = rθ or A = ½r²θ
- Forgetting to convert degrees to radians
- Calculator in wrong mode

2. Unit Inconsistency:
- Mixing units (cm and m, etc.)
- Wrong units in final answer
- Not converting RPM to rad/s

3. Formula Misuse:
- Using wrong formula (arc length vs. sector area)
- Forgetting ½ in sector area formula
- Using diameter instead of radius

4. Velocity Errors:
- Confusing linear and angular velocity
- Wrong relationship (v = ω/r instead of v = rω)
- Not converting RPM to rad/s for ω

5. Interpretation Errors:
- Not checking if answer makes physical sense
- Wrong sign or direction for velocity
- Not considering real-world constraints

Error Prevention:
- Always check angle mode (degrees vs. radians)
- Convert to radians before using formulas
- Verify units are consistent throughout
- Memorize formulas: s = rθ, A = ½r²θ, v = rω
- Check if answer is reasonable (order of magnitude)
- Interpret result in context of problem
key_concept.context: Circular measurement errors are common—radian/degree confusion is the biggest. Using degrees in s = rθ gives answers 57× too large (180/π ≈ 57). Unit inconsistencies give wrong magnitudes. Formula misuse (forgetting ½ in sector area) gives answers 2× wrong. Velocity errors (confusing v and ω) give completely wrong interpretations. Understanding these errors develops circular measurement literacy—knowing when to use which formula, how to convert units, what answers should look like. In engineering, errors mean wrong material estimates. In physics, errors mean wrong motion predictions. This section teaches error awareness—critical for accurate circular measurement work.

image.caption: Figure 7.2.27: Diagram showing common circular measurement errors and how to avoid them.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Radian/Degree Error

image.caption: Figure 7.2.28: Correcting errors from using degrees instead of radians.

worked_examples.problem: A student calculated arc length for r = 10 cm, θ = 60° as s = 10 × 60 = 600 cm. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.calculation:
Student used degrees in s = rθ formula
Formula requires radians

Correct: 10.47 cm vs. Wrong: 600 cm
Wrong answer is 57× too large (180/π ≈ 57)
Always convert degrees to radians before using s = rθ or A = ½r²θ

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: used degrees instead of radians; Correct: 10π/3 cm ≈ 10.47 cm
worked_examples.concept_applied: Arc length and sector area formulas require radians; convert degrees to radians first.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Unit Inconsistency

image.caption: Figure 7.2.29: Correcting errors from inconsistent units.

worked_examples.problem: A student calculated sector area for r = 5 cm, θ = π/4 as A = ½(5)²(π/4) = 9.82. Identify the error and correct it.

step_by_step.calculation:
Calculation is correct numerically
But answer missing units!

step_by_step.explanation: Area should be in square units.

step_by_step.calculation: r in cm → r² in cm² → A in cm² ✓

step_by_step.explanation: Mixing cm and m in same calculation.
step_by_step.calculation:
If r = 5 cm but answer expected in m²:
Convert r to meters first: r = 0.05 m
Then A = ½(0.05)²(π/4) = 0.000982 m²

Always include units in answer
Ensure all measurements use same units

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: missing units; Correct: 25π/8 cm² ≈ 9.82 cm²
worked_examples.concept_applied: Always include units in answers; ensure consistent units throughout calculation.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Velocity Relationship Error

image.caption: Figure 7.2.30: Correcting errors in linear/angular velocity relationship.

worked_examples.problem: A student calculated linear velocity as v = ω/r instead of v = rω. For r = 0.5 m, ω = 4 rad/s, they got v = 8 m/s. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.calculation:
Student used v = ω/r (wrong)
Correct formula: v = rω

v = rω (linear velocity = radius × angular velocity)
Larger radius means faster linear speed for same ω

Correct: 2 m/s vs. Wrong: 8 m/s
Wrong answer is 4× too large (r² instead of r)

v = rω: m × rad/s = m/s ✓
v = ω/r: rad/s ÷ m = rad/(m·s) ✗ (not velocity units)
Use v = rω (not ω/r); check units to verify formula

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: used v = ω/r instead of v = rω; Correct: v = 2 m/s
worked_examples.concept_applied: Linear velocity: v = rω (not ω/r); check units to verify correct formula.

image.caption: Figure 7.2.31: Summary of common errors in arc length, sector area, and velocity calculations.

sections.introduction: Apply all skills related to arc length, sector area, and velocity—formulas, conversions, applications, and error avoidance—to demonstrate mastery. These problems integrate multiple concepts and prepare students for trigonometric functions and calculus.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Practice Set

image.caption: Figure 7.2.32: Practice problems covering arc length, sector area, and velocity.

worked_examples.problem:
Complete the following problems:

1) Arc length: r = 6 cm, θ = π/2 rad.
2) Sector area: r = 10 in, θ = π/3 rad.
3) Convert 120° to radians.
4) Arc length: r = 5 m, θ = 90°.
5) Sector area: r = 8 cm, θ = 45°.
6) Linear velocity: r = 0.3 m, ω = 5 rad/s.
7) Angular velocity: 60 RPM to rad/s.
8) Arc length for 3 revolutions, r = 2 ft.
9) Sector area fraction: θ = π/4 of full circle.
10) True/False: s = rθ works with degrees.
11) Find θ if s = 10 cm, r = 5 cm.
12) Find r if A = 20π cm², θ = π/2.
13) Bicycle: r = 30 cm, 2 rev/s, find speed in m/s.
14) Sprinkler: r = 12 ft, θ = 150°, find area.
15) True/False: v = r/ω.
16) Gear A: r = 4 cm, θ = π rad; Gear B: r = 6 cm, find θ_B.
17) Satellite: r = 7000 km, θ = 0.05 rad, find distance.
18) Pizza: diameter 14 in, 8 slices, find slice area.
19) Why must θ be in radians for these formulas?
20) What is relationship between v and ω?

step_by_step.step: 13) Bicycle: r=30cm, 2 rev/s, speed in m/s

step_by_step.explanation: Convert θ to radians, use A = ½r²θ.

step_by_step.step: 16) Gear A: r=4, θ=π; Gear B: r=6, find θ_B
18) Pizza: diameter 14 in, 8 slices, slice area
step_by_step.explanation: Full area divided by 8, or sector area with θ = π/4.

step_by_step.step: 19) Why must θ be in radians
step_by_step.calculation: Formulas derived from fraction θ/2π; radians make fraction work without conversion factors

step_by_step.step: 20) What is relationship between v and ω
step_by_step.calculation: v = rω (linear velocity = radius × angular velocity)

worked_examples.final_answer: 1) 3π cm; 2) 50π/3 in²; 3) 2π/3 rad; 4) 5π/2 m; 5) 8π cm²; 6) 1.5 m/s; 7) 2π rad/s; 8) 12π ft; 9) 1/8; 10) False; 11) 2 rad; 12) 4√5 cm; 13) 1.2π m/s; 14) 60π ft²; 15) False; 16) 2π/3 rad; 17) 350 km; 18) 49π/8 in²; 19) Formulas derived with radians; 20) v = rω
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive practice demonstrating mastery of arc length, sector area, and velocity formulas and applications.

image.caption: Figure 7.2.33: Detailed solutions to all practice problems.

summary: Arc length: s = rθ (θ in radians). Sector area: A = ½r²θ (θ in radians). Linear velocity: v = rω. Angular velocity: ω = θ/t. Key requirement: θ must be in radians for all formulas—convert degrees to radians first (× π/180). Applications: wheel distance, sprinkler coverage, gear systems, satellite orbits, bicycle speed. Common errors: using degrees instead of radians, unit inconsistencies, wrong formula, confusing v and ω. Always verify units are consistent, check if answer makes physical sense, interpret results in context. Arc length and sector area formulas derived from fraction of circle (θ/2π). Radians are 'natural' angle measure because they simplify these formulas—no extra conversion factors needed. Understanding these concepts builds foundation for calculus (arc length of curves), physics (rotational motion), and real-world problem-solving in engineering, navigation, and everyday life. Essential for pre-calculus and applied mathematics.
